coredata.dc:title: Bright green phosphorescent carbon dots prepared from natural vitamins for information encryption
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Mingming
Yan, Zhongxin
Wang, Luyao
Xu, Zhengping
Zhang, Fan
Li, Li
coredata.dc:description: Materials with room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) have long lifetimes and large Stokes shifts, making them excellent candidates for bioimaging, anticounterfeiting, and encryption applications. As a metal-free RTP material, carbon dots (CDs), have received considerable attention. Using vitamin B9 (V) and ethylenediamine (EDA) as raw materials, blue fluorescent VCDs are synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal process in our study. A boric acid (BA) matrix is then used to embed the CDs, and VCDs@BA is prepared as an environmentally friendly and economical RTP material. Bright blue fluorescence and vivid green phosphorescence are released by VCDs@BA. The phosphorescence may last up to 6.0 s and has a lifespan of 311 ms. The photoluminescence quantum yield of the material is as high as 11.03%. VCDs@BA has been utilized effectively in applications for information security and anti-counterfeiting because of its optic property. This research offers a sustainable method for the synthesis of RTP materials, which is a feasible way to create room-temperature phosphorescent materials with excellent efficacy and prolonged afterglow durations.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Vitamin B9
CDs
Boric acid
Room temperature phosphorescent (RTP)
Information encryption

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM of VCDs. (b) Dispersion of particle sizes in VCDs. (c) Patterns of VCDs from XRD. (d) Absorption of UV–vis by VCDs. (e) VCD PL emission spectra in aqueous solution. (f) Time decay of fluorescence lifetime in VCDs.

(a) PL emission spectra of VCDs@BA. (b) Afterglow emission spectra of VCDs@BA. (c) RTP lifetime decay of VCDs@BA under excitation at 365 nm. (d) Photographs of VCDs@BA with the 365 nm UV lamp on and off.
(a) XPS spectra of VCDs@BA. (b) HR XPS C1s. (c) HR XPS B1s (d) VCDs@BA schematic diagram of the emission process of a composite.
(a) XRD spectra of VCDs@BA. (b) FTIR spectra of VCDs@BA.
(a) Three modal variations for VCDs@BA. (b) Digital encryption applications of VCDs@BA. (c) VCDs@BA image protection and encryption application.
Schematic of the synthetic pathway of VCDs@BA

head.ce:title: Bright green phosphorescent carbon dots prepared from natural vitamins for information encryption

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Division of Life Sciences and Medicine
University of Science and Technology of China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Materials with room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) have long lifetimes and large Stokes shifts, making them excellent candidates for bioimaging, anticounterfeiting, and encryption applications. As a metal-free RTP material, carbon dots (CDs), have received considerable attention. Using vitamin B9 (V) and ethylenediamine (EDA) as raw materials, blue fluorescent VCDs are synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal process in our study. A boric acid (BA) matrix is then used to embed the CDs, and VCDs@BA is prepared as an environmentally friendly and economical RTP material. Bright blue fluorescence and vivid green phosphorescence are released by VCDs@BA. The phosphorescence may last up to 6.0 s and has a lifespan of 311 ms. The photoluminescence quantum yield of the material is as high as 11.03%. VCDs@BA has been utilized effectively in applications for information security and anti-counterfeiting because of its optic property. This research offers a sustainable method for the synthesis of RTP materials, which is a feasible way to create room-temperature phosphorescent materials with excellent efficacy and prolonged afterglow durations.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to their vast potential in optoelectronics [1–3], sensing [4], imaging [5], and secure encryption [6,7], RTP materials are gaining in popularity. Currently, the most effective RTP materials are metal-doped inorganic and organometallic compounds [8]. Typically made of rare metals, these materials are excessively expensive, cytotoxic, stiff, and unsteady [9,10]. The intersystem crossing (ISC) rates of organic RTP materials are low and the deactivation rates are fast, rendering them inadequate alternatives [11]. Consequently, the development of innovative, metal-free RTP materials with low toxicity, environmental friendliness, extended lifespans, and easy, cost-effective procedures is of the utmost importance [12,13].

Because of their low toxicity, biocompatibility, simplicity of production and zero-dimensional optical features, carbon dots (CDs) have become increasingly popular as zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials in recent years [14–16]. Nevertheless, trying to make phosphorescent CDs at ambient temperature is difficult due to the instability of excited triplet species, the suppression of phosphorescence by oxygen, and the failure of intersystem crossover (ISC) [17,18]. Several processes seem to be involved in the phosphorescence of carbon dots at normal temperature. Adding heteroatoms, for example, provides an effective connection between spin and limited potential for breaking into singlet and triplet states [19–21]. Creative encoding strategies have been implemented employing fluorine-nitrogen codoped carbon dots (FNCDs) due to their emission of pH-stabilized blue fluorescence and pH-responsive green self-protected RTP [22], both of which are produced by a single-step solvent heat treatment. In addition, CDs may also be embedded in boric acid [23,24]，polyacrylamide [25], polyvinyl alcohol [26,27], fluoride [28], silica [29–31], cyanuric acid [32], and zeolite [33] among others. In recent years, by confining water-soluble carbon nanodots to the nanospace of the silica cladding layer and isolating them from the surrounding oxygen in aqueous solution, the nonradiative deactivation of the triplet exciton was minimized [34]. The phosphorescent lifetime of CNDs in aqueous solution is 1.86 s, and the quantum yield is 11.6%. Successful stabilization of the triplet excited states of CDs is attributable to the formation of robust covalent and hydrogen bonds between the rigid structure and CDs.

The energy gap between the singlet and triplet states may be lowered by absorbing electrons, as is the case with boron (B) [23]. As a result, the emission of triplet excitons is boosted when boric acid is incorporated onto CDs to aid in the interstitial space charge (ISC) shift from state 1 to state 1 (phosphorescence). In summary, RTP emission may occur through three distinct mechanisms when BA is used as a substrate [35,36]. First, boric acid serves as a solid matrix, and the glassy state it creates prohibits the use of the CD triple exciton outside of radiometric measurement. In addition, boron's vacant p orbital makes it an electron-attractive atom, which might lead to transitions that create a p conjugate system, thus tightening the gap between the S1 and T1 energies and boosting the ISC speed. More importantly, the combination is stabilized by the covalent bond formed between the B and C atoms [37]. Given its advantages, BA has been praised for its potential as a matrix for producing RTP content on CDs. While boric acid has been studied for its phosphorescent emission properties, the majority of these investigations have included the usage of hazardous chemicals such as organic small molecules in the creation of carbon dots.

Here, we screened an environmentally friendly [38], nontoxic, and relatively inexpensive natural vitamin B9 (VB9) as a precursor and produced blue luminous CDs using a straightforward hydrothermal approach (Scheme 1 ). In the following steps, boric acid (BA) was used to encase the CDs, and the pyrolysis process yielded an RTP material called VCDs@BA that is both cost-effective and kind to the environment. The photoluminescence (PL) and phosphorescence they exhibit is remarkable. The RTP lifespan of VCDs@BA is 311 ms, and afterglow may be seen for up to 6 s. The photoluminescence quantum yield of the material is as high as 11.03%. Considering the excellent quality of VCDs@BA's RTP, they have found widespread usage in data-safety and counterfeit prevention. Therefore, VCDs@BA is a sensible way to produce URTP materials with high efficiency and a long afterglow lifetime since it can be simply synthesized utilizing readily accessible, environmentally acceptable, and cost-effective chemicals.
Titan Scientific (Shanghai, China) was the vendor for Vitamin B9 (VB9), ethylenediamine (EDA), and boric acid (BA). The whole experiment was conducted using distilled water.

Images were taken using a TECNAI G2 microscope and a 100 kV accelerating voltage for high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) (FEI, USA). The VERTEX 70 FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) was used to acquire the FTIR spectra. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were obtained with the help of an ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (ThermoFisher, USA). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were produced using a Rigaku Minister instrument (Tokyo, Japan). The UV absorption spectra were collected using a Hitachi UV 2450 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). Fluorescence spectra were acquired utilizing an F97Pro FL spectrophotometer coupled to a 1.0 cm quartz cell (Lengguang Technology, Shanghai). Using a FLS-1000 fluorescence spectrophotometer, the fluorescent and phosphorescent lifetimes were determined at room temperature (Edinburgh, UK).

In a typical synthesis of VCDs, 100 mg of V was dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water and mixed well. Next, 300 μL of ethylenediamine (EDA) was added to the solution, which was then mixed using an ultrasonic device for 10 min to ensure a uniform distribution. The solution was transferred to a 50 mL flask lined with poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (PTFE) and dried in an oven at 180 °C for 8 h. After the reaction was complete, the solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min to separate out the large particles, and then filtered using a needle filter (e.g., 0.2 μm pore size) to remove any remaining impurities. Prior to the experiment, it was ensured that all glassware and equipment used in this procedure were clean and dry.

Generally, 50 μL, 100 μL, 200 μL, or 300 μL of the VCDs solution was transferred to a small beaker. Then, 20 mL of ultrapure water was added to the beaker and thoroughly mixed. Next, 3 g of BA was added to the solution and mixed again. The mixture was baked at 180 °C for 5 h to dry the material. After cooling to room temperature, composites with a distinct amorphous and glassy structure were obtained. The composites were subsequently ground into a fine powder for further use. To ensure experimental accuracy, all glassware and equipment were cleaned and dried prior to use.

The morphology and particle distributions of VCDs were studied by TEM, and the results show that the particles are uniformly spherical and, on average, 2.7 nm in size (Fig. 1 b). As with graphite carbon, the lattice fringe spacing in VCDs is 0.21 nm (Fig. 1a) [39]. The XRD results for the VCDs are shown in Fig. 1c. Peak values are notably distinct, indicating that the VCDs were manufactured effectively. Consistent spectroscopic analyses were carried out to learn more about the photophysical behavior of VCDs. The UV–vis absorption spectra are shown in Fig. 1d. Two humps, one at 279 nm and the other at 353 nm, formed as a consequence of UV absorption; these peaks were attributed to the n-π* transition of C–O and CO or C–N functional groups found in the periphery of the VCDs [39]. As a result of including these functional groups, the CDs' fluorescence properties were enhanced. This can be observed in the inset of Fig. 1e, as the excitation wavelength is steadily increased from 365 to 465 nm, the fluorescence wavelength is moved to the redder end of the spectrum, from 445 to 530 nm. An excitation-dependent behavior appears to be occurring here. The results show that 400 nm is the peak excitation wavelength and 472 nm is the peak emission wavelength. As determined by the device, the absolute quantum yield of VCDs is 4.68%. The decline in the fluorescence lifetime, shown in Fig. 1f, takes place over 7.01 ns (Table S1).

In aqueous solutions, the phosphorescence of nanoparticles tends to be quenched due to the nonradiative deactivation of triplet excitons by the oxygen present in the water. The fluorescence spectrum of VCDs@BA in aqueous solution is presented in Fig. S4. No phosphorescent emission was observed in the aqueous solution of VCDs [32,34,39]. The UV–Vis absorption spectra are depicted in Fig. S5, indicating the presence of two humps: one at 232 nm and the other at 347 nm, resulting from UV absorption. These peaks are attributed to the n-π* transition of functional groups, such as C–O and CO or C–N, located in the periphery of VCDs@BA. Fascinatingly, when VCDs are put into a boric acid (BA) solution, the resulting compound, VCDs@BA, emits a strong fluorescence and a vivid afterglow. Under a variety of illumination conditions, VCDs@BAs also show strong fluorescence and a noticeable afterglow. After being exposed to UV light, the VCDs@BA complex emits green phosphorescent light for approximately 6 s after the excitation light is turned off (Fig. 2 d). As shown in Fig. 2c, the average VCDs-200@BA lifetime is 311 ms (Table S2) when excited at 365 nm, and its decay can be characterized using a triexponential function. According to these results, VCDs@BA excitation-dependent features may be due to the occurrence of several triple-excited states. Afterglow is not seen in the solution, which is indicative of RTP's ability to retain performance in the solid state. Table S3 illustrates the results of our comparisons of our study's findings with those of previous studies focusing on RTP lifespan to better comprehend our own findings.

For the most luminous afterglow, the VCDs to BA ratio was maximized while making VCDs@BA composites. As shown in Fig. 2a, when exposed to UV 365 nm irradiation, the fluorescence spectra of VCDs-200@BA composites exhibit a maximum emission peak at 440 nm, indicating blue light emission, with a fluorescence emission peak similar to that of VCDs in aqueous solution. Furthermore, Fig. 2b depicts the solid phosphorescence spectrum of the VCDs-200@BA composite under stimulation at a UV wavelength of 365 nm, with a maximum emission at 534, which is indicative of green spectral emission. In comparison to the emission of the fluorescence spectrum, the emission of the phosphorescence spectrum is approximately 94 nm redshifted. These results demonstrate that our material can successfully generate both blue fluorescence and green phosphorescence simultaneously, providing strong evidence for this claim.

A variety of controlled investigations were performed to obtain in order to have a deeper knowledge of RTP. When either VCDs or BA was used as a precursor on its own, no RTP was found. Afterward, 3 g of BA was reacted with either 50 μL, 100 μL, or 300 μL of VCDs under the same circumstances to produce VCDs@BA designated VCDs-50@BA, VCDs-100@BA, and VCDs-300@BA. Maximum fluorescence emission wavelengths were measured at 438 nm, 439 nm, and 440 nm for VCDs-50@BA, VCDs-100@BA, and VCDs-300@BA, respectively, when excited at 365 nm (Fig. S1a; S2a; S3a). These spectra are extremely comparable to those produced with VCDs-200@BA. Peaks in phosphorescence emission may also be seen at 530, 534, and 533 nm, as seen in Figs. S1b, S2b, and S3b. Figs. S1c, S2c, and S3c, shows the decay curves of the phosphor life, and the phosphor life is 222 ms, 247 ms and 300 ms. To further understand the luminescence effect, we tested the photoluminescence quantum yields of the materials. The quantum yields of VCDs@BA were 8.7%, 9.71%, 11.03% and 10.34%, respectively. These results suggest that VCDs-200@BA has superior fluorescence and phosphorescence capabilities.

Using XPS elemental composition analysis, the surface functional groups and chemical composition of VCDs@BA were examined further. Fig. 3 a shows the complete scan XPS spectra of VCDs@BA, which reveal atomic percentages of O (55.48%), C (9.47%), N (2.47%) and B (32.58%). There are three separate peaks, located at 532.83 eV for O1, 284.95 eV for C1, and 192.02 eV for B1s. The C1s HR XPS spectra exhibit three different features: the C–C/CC line (at 284.3 eV), the C–O line (at 285.3 eV), and the COO line (at 286.8 eV) (Fig. 3b) [23,35]. The XPS spectra of HR B1s exhibit three peaks at 192.5 eV, 193.3 eV, and 194 eV, which were ascribed to BCO2, B2O3, and B–O, respectively (Fig. 3c).

The morphology of the materials was characterized using a high-resolution electron microscope. As seen in Fig. S6, the carbon points exhibit a spherical shape and are uniformly distributed, with a particle size of less than 5 nm. Additionally, HRTEM results reveal that the material's surface possesses a crystalline structure. To learn more about the composition of the VCDs@BA composites, XRD and FTIR investigations were carried out. Different VCDs@BA content composites show FT-IR spectral absorption bands that are qualitatively comparable to those found for the VCDs themselves Fig. 4 a shows that the modest stretching vibration of CC causes an expansive absorption peak at 2263 cm−1. However, the highest absorption occurs at 3214 cm−1, which is due to the O–H stretching vibration. Upon further spectroscopic examination, two prominent peaks were found: one at 1440 cm−1 (indicating asymmetric CH stretching vibrations) and another at 1188 cm−1 (corresponding to symmetric C–O–C stretching vibrations) [25,26]. Absorption bands in the FT-IR spectrum for VCDs@BA composites with varying VCDs@BA concentrations are almost similar to those of VCDs. This suggests that the VCDs are not covalently bonded to the surface, but rather are randomly distributed inside the matrix. In terms of XRD patterns, there is essentially no difference between the four different VCDs@BAs. At 2ϴ = 14.33°, 27.88°, and 39.94°, three distinct peaks, which corresponded to the usual peaks of BA, were discovered (Fig. 4b) [23]. The XPS and FTIR measurements provided identical findings [40].

A reasonable mechanism for VCDs@BAs phosphorescence can be proposed based on the above observations (Fig. 3d) [23,41]. For starters, the C = O bonds in VCDs@BA may cause RTP to form. As previously mentioned, VCDs exhibit two peak absorption bands at 255 and 350 nm, which are attributed to the π→π* transition of CC and the n→π* transition of CO, respectively. When VCDs are combined with BA, fluorescence emission occurs upon 365 nm excitation. The excitation spectrum of VCDs@BA overlaps with its absorption spectrum, indicating that the reverse intersystem crossing (RTP) mechanism primarily originates from the CO bond of VCDs. Additionally, BA is required because VCDs alone cannot emit phosphorescence. When VCDs are incorporated into the glassy BA state, a new boron-carbon bond is formed between the VCDs and the BA matrix. This is confirmed by the FT-IR stretching vibration of VCDs@BA at 950 cm−1, which confirms the presence of the C–B bond [40,42]. We hypothesize that these covalent bonds, combined with the glassy state, can effectively immobilize the tristate species (i.e., the CO-containing portion) to VCDs. This, in turn, protects the excited tristate excitons from being quenched and promotes RTP emission. Moreover, it has been observed that VCDs@BA composites fail to exhibit RTP upon redispersion in water. This can be attributed to the hydrolysis of BA in water, which results in the formation of boric acid [37]. The subsequent release of VCDs from the BA grid causes the C–B bond in the composite to break, thereby causing the afterglow emission to vanish. Supporting this assertion, the fluorescence emission spectra of VCDs@BA composites redispersed in aqueous solution demonstrate consistency with the spectra of the original CDs. (refer to Fig. S4). This serves to further corroborate that the VCDs are released from the BA grid [36,43].

In addition, boron doping exhibits unique properties. Boron atoms have an empty p orbital, which can attract π electrons to form a p-π * conjugated system. This reduces the minimum unoccupied energy level of the system. The following calculations confirm this conclusion. The energy gap (EST) between the lowest single state (S1) and triplet state (T1) of VCDs@BA at 365 nm was calculated to be approximately 0.49 eV by analyzing the photoluminescence (PL) and phosphorescence emission spectra of the material (Table S4). Furthermore, these numbers are minimal enough to facilitate a successful ISC procedure. Below is the equation used in the computation [22,44].
EST = h/k*C/λ = 1240/λ

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application in anti-counterfeiting, information security and image protection
ce:section.ce:para: VCDs@BA's inexpensive price, low environmental effect, and highly efficient blue fluorescence with green phosphorescent emission make it a promising contender for anti-counterfeiting and information security applications. First, VCDs@BA was used as a model to show their possible uses as intelligent materials in the field of security protection. The formation of the VCDs@BA powder into a variety of patterns, such as those depicted in Fig. 5 a, is an example of a typical use of the three-mode switch encryption method. The patterns appear white when exposed to the daylight. The patterns emit blue fluorescence when subjected to ultraviolet light with a wavelength of 365 nm. After turning off the UV light, a pattern of phosphorescence that is bright green becomes visible.

In addition, VCDs@BA can be used for digital encryption because of its excellent green phosphorescent emission when the light is switched off. As illustrated in Fig. 5b, VCDs@BA and other powder materials are used to create the “8888″ number die. The four numerals we have encrypted are almost impossible to distinguish in broad daylight, since they are all white and display the sequence “8888". The four numerals fluoresce a strong shade of blue when a 365 nm UV light is shined on the powdered digital molds, and the numbers continue to read “8888″ even after the lamp is turned off. After the excitation light is turned off, the numerals “2022″ are plainly discernible as brilliant green phosphorescence. Prior to that, they appeared in the same location as the letters “8888". The findings shown above illustrate the usefulness of VCDs@BA in a variety of sophisticated applications involving information and digital security.

Fig. 5c demonstrates the applications of VCDs@BA for image protection and graphic security. We have used VCDs@BA and other nonphosphorescent powder materials of different colors to produce vivid patterns of sunflowers, snowmen's heads and heart-shaped flowers; the sunflower pattern has several beautiful petals of different colors in daylight, the white petals change color to blue when excited by the UV lamp and, happily, only four green petals appear when the excitation light is switched off. The beautiful snowman head shows a red hat and white facial outline in daylight; and turning the excitation light on to the snowman image shows a blue facial outline; turning the excitation light off makes the hat and facical outline disappear, leaving only the green smiley face expression. The five-petalled heart-shaped flower shows similar results. Before the excitation light is switched off, there are five different colored petals; after the excitation light is switched off only two green petals prepared from our phosphorescent material appear. Based on these findings, it is clear that VCDs@BA may be used effectively for image protection and security.

We provide a straightforward and cost-effective approach for preparing high-efficiency phosphorescent materials. Blue VCDs with high quantum rates are formed when natural VB9 is hydrothermally decomposed in water. At room temperature and in the presence of a boric acid matrix, the VCDs@BA composites emit a bright green phosphorescence, with a peak emission wavelength of 534 nm and an afterglow lifetime of 311 ms. Phosphorescence may be observed for up to 6 s. The photoluminescence quantum yield of the material is as high as 11.03%. The spectacular phosphorescence of VCDs@BA may be attributed in large part to the presence of glassy empty p-orbitals of boron oxide, which play a crucial role in the onset of ISC and the stability of the triplet state in these materials. This material's phosphorescence qualities make it useful for a wide variety of applications, including data encryption, double security, and picture protection.

MingMing Cheng: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Zhongxin Yan: Supervision, Software, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Luyao Wang: Methodology, Data curation. Zhengping Xu: Methodology, Data curation, Validation. Fan Zhang: Software, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Li Li: Software, Data curation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 62027825, 82172077, and 22201298), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR2019MC069, ZR2022QB077, and ZR2022QB243), the Innovation and Entrepreneurship Team of Jiangsu Province (JSSCTD202145), Science and Technology Innovation Team Project of Foshan (2018IT100382), and the Youth Innovation Promotion Association of CAS (2022326).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2023.119888.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multicolor-Emissive carbon dots for white-light-emitting diodes and room-temperature phosphorescence

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Kumari, R.; Kumar, A.; Negi, K.; Sahu, S.K. Multicolor-Emissive Carbon Dots for White-Light-Emitting Diodes and Room-Temperature Phosphorescence. ACS Applied Nano Materials 2023, doi:10.1021/acsanm.2c04312.

Song, S.Y.; Liu, K.K.; Cao, Q.; Mao, X.; Zhao, W.B.; Wang, Y.; Liang, Y.C.; Zang, J.H.; Lou, Q.; Dong, L.; et al. Ultraviolet phosphorescent carbon nanodots. Light Sci Appl 2022, 11, 146, doi:10.1038/s41377-022-00837-1.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Exceeding 67.35% efficient and color temperature tunable white light from carbon dots with quadruple-channel fluorescence–phosphorescence emission

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Shen, J.; Xu, B.; Chen, S.; Jia, Y.; Li, J.; Jiang, T.; Gao, Z.; Wang, X.; Zhu, C.; Shi, H.; et al. Exceeding 67.35% Efficient and Color Temperature Tunable White Light from Carbon Dots with Quadruple-Channel Fluorescence-Phosphorescence Emission. ACS Sustainable Chemistry & Engineering 2022, 10, 15599-15607, doi:10.1021/acssuschemeng.2c05268.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Long-lived room temperature phosphorescence crystals with green light excitation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zheng, Y.; Wang, Z.; Liu, J.; Zhang, Y.; Gao, L.; Wang, C.; Zheng, X.; Zhou, Q.; Yang, Y.; Li, Y.; et al. Long-Lived Room Temperature Phosphorescence Crystals with Green Light Excitation. ACS Appl Mater Interfaces 2022, 14, 15706-15715, doi:10.1021/acsami.2c04141.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Near-infrared phosphorescent carbon dots for sonodynamic precision tumor therapy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Geng, B.; Hu, J.; Li, Y.; Feng, S.; Pan, D.; Feng, L.; Shen, L. Near-infrared phosphorescent carbon dots for sonodynamic precision tumor therapy. Nat Commun 2022, 13, 5735, doi:10.1038/s41467-022-33474-8.
